questions.question_text: Every Ohio brokerage must maintain a trust or special account at what type of institution?
distractors: Any U.S. federally insured bank
A Division-approved national bank only
The state treasury department
questions.explanation: Ohio law requires the trust or special account to be at a recognized Ohio depository. The account must be designated as a trust or special account on all bank records and checks.

questions.question_text: A broker receives a $10,000 earnest money deposit on Friday afternoon. When must the funds be deposited into the trust account?
distractors: Within 5 business days
By the following Monday at noon
Within 30 days of the contract date
questions.explanation: Ohio law requires all trust funds (earnest money, security deposits, etc.) to be deposited promptly upon receipt. There is no specific multi-day grace period specified.

questions.question_text: A broker places $500 of personal funds in the brokerage trust account to cover bank fees. This is:
distractors: Acceptable for maintaining minimum balances
Permitted if the amount is under $1,000
Required to keep the account operational
questions.explanation: Commingling broker personal funds with client trust funds is prohibited in Ohio. The trust account must contain only client funds and funds belonging to others.

questions.question_text: How must a brokerage's trust account be labeled on bank records in Ohio?
distractors: Using only the broker's personal name
With a standard business checking designation
With any name the broker prefers
questions.explanation: Ohio requires the account to be specifically designated as a 'trust account' or 'special account' on all bank records and checks, clearly distinguishing it from the broker's operating accounts.

questions.question_text: An Ohio broker has one trust account that holds earnest money from sales transactions and security deposits from rentals. Is this acceptable?
questions.correct_answer: No, a separate PM trust account is required
distractors: Yes, one trust account can hold all funds
Yes, if the funds are tracked in separate ledgers
Only if the broker manages fewer than 10 properties
questions.explanation: Ohio requires a separate trust account for property management activities. Sales earnest money and PM funds (security deposits, rents) must not be combined in one account.

questions.question_text: An Ohio broker manages 15 rental properties. What type of account must be maintained for tenant security deposits?
questions.correct_answer: A separate property management trust account
distractors: The broker's regular business operating account
The same trust account used for sales escrow
Individual accounts for each tenant
questions.explanation: Ohio requires brokers engaged in property management to maintain a separate trust account for PM activities, keeping security deposits and rents segregated from sales trust funds.

questions.question_text: In an Ohio property management trust account, how should different types of funds be handled?
questions.correct_answer: Funds must be segregated by purpose
distractors: All PM funds can be pooled together freely
Only security deposits need separate tracking
Funds are segregated only at year-end for accounting
questions.explanation: Funds in the PM trust account must be segregated by purpose — security deposits, rents, and owner funds should be tracked separately to ensure proper accounting.

questions.question_text: A property manager collects first month's rent and a security deposit from a new tenant. Where should these funds be placed?
questions.correct_answer: The brokerage's property management trust account
distractors: The property owner's personal bank account
The broker's general operating account
The sales trust account with earnest money
questions.explanation: All funds received in the course of property management — including rent and security deposits — must be deposited into the separate PM trust account maintained by the brokerage.

questions.question_text: An Ohio PM broker transfers a property owner's rental income from the PM trust account to the owner. What documentation must be maintained?
questions.correct_answer: Records of the disbursement for Division audit
distractors: No documentation is needed for owner disbursements
Only an email confirmation to the owner
A form filed with the Division within 30 days
questions.explanation: All trust account transactions, including disbursements to property owners, must be documented and retained. Records must be available for Division audit at any time.

questions.question_text: A broker manages a mixed portfolio of commercial and residential rentals. How many property management trust accounts are needed?
questions.correct_answer: At least one separate PM trust account
distractors: One account per property type is mandatory
One account per individual property is required
No separate account if under 20 properties
questions.explanation: Ohio requires at least one separate PM trust account for all property management activities. While more accounts may be advisable, the law requires at minimum a separate PM account.

questions.question_text: How long must an Ohio broker retain trust account records?
questions.correct_answer: 3 years from closing or listing termination
distractors: 1 year from the transaction date
5 years from the date of deposit
7 years as required by federal tax law
questions.explanation: Brokers must retain all transaction records, including trust account records, for 3 years from the date of closing or termination of the listing agreement.

questions.question_text: Which records must an Ohio broker maintain for trust account compliance?
distractors: Only monthly bank statements
Only deposit receipts and checks
Only the annual reconciliation summary
questions.explanation: Complete trust account records include receipts, disbursements, bank statements, and reconciliations. All must be maintained and available for Division audit.

questions.question_text: The Division of Real Estate decides to audit a broker's trust account. Must the broker be given advance notice?
questions.correct_answer: No, the Division can audit at any time
distractors: Yes, 30 days written notice is required
Yes, 10 business days notice minimum
Only with a court order or subpoena
questions.explanation: The Division has the authority to audit broker records at any time, without advance notice. Brokers must keep records current and accessible for inspection.

questions.question_text: A broker's salesperson handles trust account deposits. An error is discovered during a Division audit. Who is held responsible?
questions.correct_answer: The broker, even if delegated to a salesperson
distractors: Only the salesperson who made the error
Both equally under Ohio law
Neither, if the error was unintentional
questions.explanation: The broker is responsible for the trust account even when day-to-day management is delegated to a salesperson. The broker must ensure proper handling and supervision.

questions.question_text: An Ohio broker closed a transaction two years ago and wants to shred the trust account records from that sale. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, records must be kept for at least 3 years
distractors: Yes, 2 years is sufficient retention time
Yes, if the records have been digitized
Only if both parties consent to destruction
questions.explanation: Ohio requires 3-year retention from closing or listing termination. Destroying records before the 3-year period expires violates the record-keeping requirement.
